summary: Gruber critiques the Safari 15 redesign across iOS and Mac, arguing it sacrifices usability for the appearance of simplicity. On iOS, the new bottom-positioned tab bar buries frequently-used controls like Share and Bookmarks behind a catch-all '···' menu, while the swipe-to-switch-tabs feature offers marginal benefit. On Mac, the merged tab/address bar creates disorienting movement, wastes horizontal space, and hides controls unnecessarily. He quotes Nick Heer's concept of 'chickenshit minimalism' — hiding complexity behind vague controls rather than making real design choices — and concludes that Apple's HI team is removing the coherence between how apps look and what they actually do.
key_points: The iOS redesign buries Share and Bookmarks — frequently used one-tap actions — inside a generic '···' menu, trading real utility for visual minimalism
The Mac tab redesign causes disorienting movement as the location bar shifts with each tab change, wastes horizontal space, and is inconsistent with every other tabbed app in macOS
The new design exemplifies 'chickenshit minimalism': the illusion of simplicity that adds complexity by hiding controls behind vague menus rather than making real hierarchy decisions
Apple's own WWDC session on 'Discoverable Design' contradicts the choices made in the Safari 15 redesign
The redesign removes bookmarklet support entirely, eliminating functionality that power users depend on
money_quotes: The new Safari tab UI strikes me as being different for the sake of being different, not different for the sake of being better.
The system-wide standard iOS/iPadOS Share popover menu is one of the best UIs Apple has come up with in the last decade.
It just looks simpler. All the old functionality remains — it's just harder to access, harder to discover intuitively, and more distracting.

One can only presume that Apple's HI team thinks they're reducing needless 'clutter', but what they're doing is systematically removing the coherence between what apps look like and the functionality they offer.
It's not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.
Long-pressing '···' to toggle Reader Mode feels like they just didn't know where else to put it.
Tabs in Safari on Mac (and, in my opinion, iPad) were a solved problem.
Good luck convincing Chrome users to switch to Safari with this design.
key_insight: Apple's Safari 15 redesign mistakes the appearance of simplicity for actual simplicity, hiding proven functionality behind vague controls and breaking the fundamental design principle that how something looks should reflect how it works.
